q:
With reference to carbon nanotubes, consider the following statements:
1. They can be used as carriers of drugs and antigens in the human body.
2. They can be made into artificial blood capillaries for an injured part of human body.
3. They can be used in biochemical sensors.
4. Carbon nanotubes are biodegradable.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
expl: Figure: Carbon nanotubes Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are cylindrical molecules that consist of rolled-up sheets of graphene (single-layer carbon atoms). These carbon nanotubes can be single-walled (SWCNT) or multi-walled (MWCNT) with a diameter of less than 1 nanometer (nm) or more than 100 nm, respectively. Multi-walled (MWCNT) nanotubes consist of several concentrically interlinked nanotubes. The length of nanotubes can reach up to several micrometers or even millimeters. These were earlier considered resistant to chemical damage and immune to biodegradation, but peroxidase enzymes were found to play an important role in the process of biodegradation of carbon nanotubes. The NASA has demonstrated the use of carbon nanotube arrays as biosensors. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are characterized by unique chemical and biological properties. These carbon nanotubes can penetrate through cell membranes, capillaries and accumulate in cells and tissues. This technology would facilitate the making of nanodevices using these blood-compatible nanomaterials as building blocks for biomedical applications such as artificial implants, including structural tissue replacements, that is, artificial blood vessels, or functional devices such as drug delivery matrixes. However using carbon nanotube as an artificial blood vessel, it can create blood clotting, and thus unsafe.

q:
Consider the following activities:
1. Spraying pesticides on a crop field
2. Inspecting the craters of active volcanoes
3. Collecting breath samples from spouting whales for DNA analysis At the present level of technology, which of the above activities can be successfully carried out by using drones?
expl: The use of drones to spray pesticides (in Telangana) was in the news. It is considered illegal in India, but is legally practised in many of the states of the USA. Drones can be used to study the craters of active volcanoes a place which human beings cannot directly access. Australian scientists used drones to collect the mucus samples of whales from their water sprays to examine their health. Drone technology can be used in emergency disease control, weather forecasting etc.

q: The experiment will employ a trio of spacecraft flying in formation in the shape of an equilateral triangle that has sides one million kilometres long, with lasers shining between the craft”. The experiment in question refers to
expl: Earth Earth’s orbital path Spacecraft 2 Laser beams (1 incoming, 1 outgoing) Solar array Thrusters Gravity waves Spacecraft 1 Interferometers Spacecraft 3 Figure: Evolved Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (eLISA) project The LIGO detector found the second detection of gravitational waves in a research experiment on black holes using gravitational waves. The evolved Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (eLISA) project is a plan of setting three spacecraft, a mother and two daughter spacecraft into space. These spacecrafts will fly in a triangular formation and trail the earth in its orbit around the sun at a distance of over 50 million km. The distance of each arm of the imaginary triangle, from the mother to each daughter spacecraft will be about a million km. Freely falling test masses (cubes) with sides measuring about 46 mm will float inside these spacecraft, and laser interferometers will measure any changes by a gravitational wave in the distance between these cubes accurately.

q:
Consider the following statements:
1. Genetic changes can be introduced in the cells that produce eggs or sperms of a prospective parent.
2. A person’s genome can be edited before birth at the early embryonic stage.
3. Human /induced pluripotent stem cells can be injected into the embryo of a pig.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
opts: 1, 2 and 3 1. This Test Booklet contains 100 items (questions). Each item comprises four responses (answers). You will select the mark the response which you consider the best. In any case, choose ONLY ONE response for each item. 2. You have to mark all your responses ONLY on the separate OMR Sheet provided. 3. All items carry equal marks. 4. Penalty for wrong answers: (i) There are four alternatives for the answer to every question. For each question for which a wrong answer has been given by the candidate, one-third (0.33) of the marks assigned to that question will be deducted as penalty. (ii) If a candidate gives more than one answer, it will be treated as a wrong answer even if one of the given answers happens to be correct and there will be same penalty as above to that question. (iii) If a question is left blank, i.e., no answer is given by the candidate, there will be no penalty for that question. General Instructions UPSC CIVIL SERVICES EXAMINATION (PRELIMS) 1 PAPER
expl: Germline gene therapy targets the reproductive cells, meaning any changes made to the DNA will be passed to the next generation. Germline gene therapy (its illegal) is when DNA is transferred into the cells that produce reproductive cells, eggs or sperm, in the body. Embryonic genetic editing is different from germline gene therapy and is possible to make genome changes to eliminate the vertical transfer of certain diseases and medical conditions. Animals bearing human cells and organs are human-animal chimeras. They offer insights into early human development and disease onset and provide a realistic drug-testing platform. Researchers, to see which cell-type would survive best, have experimented by injecting several different forms of human stem cells into pig embryos. The cells that survived longest and showed the most potential to continue to develop were intermediate human pluripotent stem cells.

q:
What is the importance of using Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccines in India?
1. These vaccines are effective against pneumonia as well as meningitis and sepsis.
2. Dependence on antibiotics that are not effective against drug-resistant bacteria can be reduced.
3. These vaccines have no side effects and cause no allergic reactions. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: Conjugate vaccine combines a weak antigen with a strong antigen as a carrier so that the immune system has a stronger A Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine (PCV) gives prevention against pneumococcal diseases caused by bacteria called Streptococcus pneumoniae or pneumococcus. Pneumococcal infections can range from ear and sinus infections to pneumonia and bloodstream infection. It can prevent septicaemia (a kind of blood poisoning, also called sepsis) and meningitis. While vaccines are not intended to replace antibiotics, they can contribute to reduce AMR (Antimicrobial Resistance or drug resistance) by preventing (resistant) bacterial diseases. PCVs for pneumonia can potentially reduce the dependence on antibiotics by nearly 50% per year. Fever, loss of appetite, headache, fussiness are some side effects of Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccines.

q: In India, the term “Public Key Infrastructure” is used in the context of
expl: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) is a technology used in the authentication of users and devices in the digital world. The main objective behind the PKI is to have one or more trusted parties digitally sign documents certifying that a particular cryptographic key belongs to a particular user or device. PKI governs encryption keys through the issuance of digital certificates, and their management, therefore verifying the owner of the private key. SSL certificates on websites are one of the most common examples of PKI.

q:
Which of the following are the reasons/ factors for exposure to benzene pollution?
1. Automobile exhaust
2. Tobacco smoke
3. Wood burning
4. Using varnished wooden furniture
5. Using products made of polyurethane Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: Benzene (C6H6) is a colourless, flammable liquid but has a sweet odour and evaporates quickly when exposed to air. It is formed naturally through volcanoes and forest fires. The most exposure to benzene is a result of human activities. Factors for exposure to benzene pollution are: 1. Automobile exhaust 2. Tobacco smoke 3. Wood burning

q:
If another global financial crisis happens in the near future, which of the following actions/policies are most likely to give some immunity to India?
1. Not depending on short-term foreign borrowings
2. Opening up to more foreign banks
3. Maintaining full capital account convertibility Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: When a government, corporation or private household borrows money from another country’s government or private lenders, then it is known as foreign debt. This debt can be a combination of short-term and long-term liabilities. Short-term foreign borrowings are more volatile than long-term debts because it can lead to liquidity crunch in the times of global crisis. Therefore, not depending on short-term foreign borrowings is most likely to give some immunity to India in times of global crisis. Foreign banks have played an instrumental role in introducing Indian customers to ATMs and credit cards. Foreign banks can play a critical role in raising money for Indian firms increasingly looking for investments overseas and connect them with a global clientele and consumers. However, Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs) can withdraw their money and park it somewhere else and create volatility and a cash crunch in the market. The freedom of foreign investors to purchase Indian assets, and that of the domestic citizens to purchase foreign financial assets is known as capital account convertibility.

q: If you withdraw ‘1, 00, 000 in cash from your Demand Deposit Account at your bank, the immediate effect on aggregate money supply in the economy will be
opts: to increase it by more than ‘1, 00, 000
expl: Money supply is the total stock of money in circulation among the public at a particular point of time, but the total stock of money is different from the total supply of money. Supply of money is only that part of the total stock of money which is held by the public at a particular point of time. The currency, printed notes, money in the deposit accounts and in the form of other liquid assets is known as the circulating money. There are four alternative measures of money supply, viz. M1, M2, M3 and M4 by the Reserve Bank of India. M1 = CU (notes plus coins held by the public) + DD (net demand deposits held by commercial banks) M2 = M1 + Savings deposits with Post Office savings banks M3 = M1 + Net time deposits of commercial banks M4 = M3 + Total deposits with Post Office savings organisations (excluding National Savings Certificates) M1 and M2 are known as narrow money while M3 and M4 are known as broad money. Their order of liquidity is: M1 > M2 > M3 > M4. M1 is the most liquid and easiest for transactions and remains unchanged. It remains unchanged because the money supply includes both cash/currency and demand deposits. Therefore, cash withdrawal from demand deposit only puts it with the public, hence no change in M.

q: With reference to the cultural history of India, which one of the following is the correct description of the term ‘paramitas’?
opts: The earliest Dharmashastra texts written in aphoristic (sutra) style.
Philosophical schools that did not accept the authority of Vedas.
Perfections whose attainment led to the Bodhisattva path.
Powerful merchant guilds of early medieval South India.
expl: Paramita or parami (in Sanskrit and Pali respectively) is a Buddhist term often translated as “perfection”. In Mahayana Buddhism, the bodhisattva practices the six paramitas, or transcendent perfections which are generosity, discipline, patience, diligence, meditative concentration and wisdom. Paramitas are described in Buddhist commentaries as noble character qualities generally associated with enlightened beings. The Ten Paramitas are: 1. Generosity 2. Morality 3. Patience 4. Energy 5. Meditation 6. Wisdom 7. Skilful means 8. Resolution 9. Power 10. Knowledge

q:
In the context of Indian history, the Rakhmabai case of 1884 revolved around
1. Women’s right to gain education
2. Age of consent
3. Restitution of conjugal rights Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: Rakhmabai (1864-1955) case of 1884 contributed to the enactment of the Age of Consent Act, 1891. After 12 years of marriage in 1885, her husband sought “restitution of conjugal rights”, so she was ordered to live with her husband or spend six months in jail. She wrote to Queen Victoria, and the Queen overruled the court’s verdict and dissolved her marriage. The Age of Consent Act, 1891, made child marriages illegal across the British Empire. Later Rakhmabai became the first women doctor to practice medicine in the British India.

q: Indigo cultivation in India declined by the beginning of the 20th century because of
opts: peasant resistance to the oppressive conduct of planters
its unprofitability in the world market because of new inventions
national leaders’ opposition to the cultivation if indigo
expl: Indigo often called “Blue Gold” is a blue-coloured dye comes from the indigo plant. It was widely-grown in India during the East India Company rule and in the British Raj and widely traded in the European market. The indigo production due to increasing demand in the 18th and 19th centuries increased significantly in Bengal and Bihar. However, by the beginning of the 20th century, a cheaper synthetic indigo which took less time during production was invented and the demand for indigo cultivation in India declined. Adolf Baeyer succeeded in synthesizing indigo in 1882. Significantly. synthetic indigo made natural indigo non-profitable for farmers as well as for traders.

q: Wellesley established the Fort William College Calcutta because
opts: he was asked by the Board of Directors at London to do so.
he wanted to revive interest in oriental learning in India.
he wanted to provide William Carey and his associates with employment.
he wanted to train British civilians for administrative purpose in India.
expl: Lord Richard Wellesley was the Governor-General of Bengal from 1798 to 1805. He established Fort William College to train British officials in 1800. The college was established in Calcutta and aimed to create civil servants who would be familiar with Indian languages, history, culture and local laws. The Court of Directors was never in support of a training college in Kolkata (Calcutta). Later, in 1807, a separate College, the East Indian Company College was established in England, but the Fort William College was the center for teaching different languages.

q: With reference to the history of India, “Ulgulan” or the Great Trumult is the description of which of the following events?
expl: Birsa Munda (1875-1900) often referred to as ‘Dharti Abba’ or the 'Earth's Father' was born in the Munda tribe which inhabited the Chota Nagpur area of the Bengal Presidency (Present-day Jharkhand). The Mundas rose under Birsa Munda (Birsa Munda's Revolt of 1899-1900) in a religious movement or rebellion (“Ulgulan”) with agrarian and political content. The objective of the movement was to establish a Munda rule in the land by killing thikadars, jagirdars, rajas and halcims. His struggle led to the passing of the Chotanagpur Tenancy Act in 1908. This act restricted the passing of land from the tribal people to non-tribals.

q:
With reference to the scholars/litterateurs of ancient India, consider the following statements:
1. Panini is associated with Pushyamitra
2. Amarasimha is associated with Harshavardhana
3. Kalidasa is associated with Chandra Gupta-II
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: Panini was an expert in language and grammar and authored one of the greatest works on grammar called Ashtadhyayi (500 BCE). He is not associated with Pushyamitra Shunga. The Shunga Empire (185 to 75 BCE) was established by Pushyamitra Shunga, after the fall of the Mauryan Empire. Amarasimha, a Sanskrit grammarian and poet, was one of the nine gems in the court of Vikramaditya (Chandragupta II). Chandragupta II reign was around 375 CE. Harshavardhana ruled North India from 606 to 647 CE. Kalidasa was a classical sanskrit writer, the greatest poet and a dramatist in the Sanskrit language of India. The earliest paleographical evidence of Kalidasa is found in a Sanskrit inscription dated c. 473 CE, found at Mandsaur’s Sun temple. Kalidasa flourished during the reign of Chandragupta II, and lived around the 4th-5th century CE. Kalidasa wrote three plays: Malavikagnimitram (Pertaining to Malavika and Agnimitra) Abhijnanasakuntalam (story of King Dushyanta and Shakuntala) Vikramorvasiyam (Urvashi Won by Valour)

q: In the context of recent advances of human reproductive technology, “Pronuclear Transfer” is used for
opts: fertilization of egg in vitro by the donor sperm.
genetic modification of sperm producing cells.
development of stem cells into functional embryos.
prevention of mitochondrial diseases in offspring.
expl: Pronuclear Transfer is the transfer of pronuclei from one zygote to another. This technique first requires fertilisation of healthy donated eggs from the mitochondrial donor with the intended male parent sperm. Simultaneously, the intending mother’s affected oocytes are fertilised with the intending father’s sperm. By ‘Maternal Spindle transfer’ technique, the maternal DNA is put into the egg of a donor woman, which is then fertilized using the father’s sperm. This technique was developed to help existing In-vitro-Fertilization (IVF) treatments in which mothers have mitochondrial diseases. Maternal DNA mutations are a cause of mitochondrial disease that can lead to premature death, sometimes in infancy or childhood.

q:
With the present state of development, Artificial Intelligence can effectively do which of the following?
1. Bring down electricity consumption in industrial units
2. Create meaningful short stories and songs
3. Disease diagnosis
4. Text-to-Speech Conversion
5. Wireless transmission of electrical energy Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: Artificial Intelligence describes the action of machines accomplishing tasks that have historically required human intelligence. It includes technologies like machine learning, pattern recognition, big data, neural networks, self-algorithms, etc. AI in buildings and factories can monitor and collect information about energy consumption in the form of numbers, text, images and videos. AI works in programmed ecosystem and it does not have emotions so it would be difficult for a meaningful short stories and songs. Google is using IOT and A.I from its deepmind acquisition to reduce energy consumption in its data centre. AI combined with robotics and the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) could potentially be the new nervous system for healthcare, presenting solutions to address healthcare problems. Integration of AI technology in cancer care could improve the accuracy and speed of diagnosis, aid clinical decision- making and lead to better health outcomes. Speech synthesis is the artificial production of human speech. It is a way to convert language to the human voice (or speech). For example, Google’s Assistant, Amazon’s Echo, Apple’s Siri, etc. AI can be used in the energy sector which include energy system modelling and forecasting to decrease unpredictability and increase efficiency in power balancing and usage. However it cannot be used for transmission of electrical energy.

q:
With reference to Visible Light Communication (VLC) technology, which of the following statements are correct?
1. VLC uses electromagnetic spectrum wavelengths 375 to 780 nm.
2. VLC is known as long-range optical wireless communication.
3. VLC can transmit large amounts of data faster than Bluetooth.
4. VLC has no electromagnetic interference. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: Visible Light Communication (VLC) Technology is an emerging technology. It intends to enable high-speed internet access primarily in the indoor environment. VLC technology has several advantages over traditional radio-frequency (RF) based networks. It uses electromagnetic spectrum wavelengths 380-780 nm that can be detected by the human eye. The illumination and data transfer simultaneously is possible to achieve by means of LEDs. It will lead to both interior lighting of a room and data transfer without an additional communication system. This technology is known as Visual Light Communication. Advanced technology allow to modulate the light at higher frequencies, allowing richer information, and can lead to transmission of light across a free space (e.g., lasers communicating between two buildings) or across a medium (e.g., fiber optics). With the advent of LED, a new idea in VLC is to use general lighting to communicate with users in a space as a replacement or supplement to Wi-Fi. While traditional light sources present practical limitations, LED lighting can be modulated at very high frequencies, with a cycle as short as nanoseconds. This concept, called Li -Fi, could be a solution to RF bandwidth limitations as the visible light spectrum is 10,000 larger than the radio spectrum. As a bonus, VLC doesn’t cause electromagnetic interference. The light can transmit information either directly or reflected from a surface. It can do so while dimmed. However, light cannot penetrate obstacles such as walls. Thus, for wireless communication purposes VLC cannot be used for long-distances. While Bluetooth transmits data at the rate of 300 kbps (kilobytes per-second), VLC can transmit data in the range between mbps- gbps (megabytes per second to gigabytes per second).

q:
With reference to “Blockchain Technology”, consider the following statements:
1. It is a public ledger that everyone can inspect, but which no single user controls.
2. The structure and design of blockchain is such that all the data in it are about cryptocurrency only.
3. Applications that depend on basic features of blockchain can be developed without anybody’s permission.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: Transactional records of the public, also known as the block, are stored in a chain, in a network connected through peer- to-peer nodes, is known as the blockchain technology. 'Digital ledger' term is used to refer to this storage. ’In simpler words, the digital ledger is like a Google spreadsheet shared among numerous computers in a network in which anybody can see the data, but they cannot corrupt it. The words “block” stands for digital information and “chain” stands for public database. Blocks store information about transactions like the date, time and dollar amount of your most recent purchase, who is participating in transactions using a unique “digital signature,” sort of like a username. Blockchains can be both permissioned and non-permissioned (permissionless). Public blockchain, typically permissionless as well, is the type associated with Bitcoin (the application depends on the basic features), where access and validation are open to participants, achieving high degree of decentralization. In private permissioned blockchain a trusted entity controls the validation and writing of data permissions.

q: A Parliamentary System of Government is one in which
opts: all political parties in the Parliament are represented in the Government.
the Government is responsible to the Parliament and can be removed by it.
the Government is elected by the people and can be removed by them.
the Government is chosen by the Parliament but cannot be removed by it before completion of a fixed term.
expl: A Parliamentary System of Government is one in which the Government is responsible to the Parliament and can be removed by it. In the Parliamentary System of Government the role of the President or monarch is primarily ceremonial, and the Prime Minister along with the cabinet wields real power. There are two executives: The nominal executive is the head of the state e.g. President while the real executive is the Prime Minister, who is the head of the government. Countries with such a system include Germany, Italy, Japan, United Kingdom as well as Portugal. The Constitution of India provides for a parliamentary form of government, both at the Centre and in the States. According to Article 75 (3), the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the House of the People (Lok Sabha). It is one of the constituents of the parliament. Any Lok Sabha MP, who can garner the support of 50 colleagues, can introduce a motion of no- confidence against the Council of Ministers. If the motion is passed in the Lok Sabha, the government falls.

q: Which part of the Constitution of India declares the ideal of Welfare State?
expl: The Part IV of the Indian Constitution (Articles 36 to 51) provides the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP). The DPSP constitutes a comprehensive social, economic and political programme for a modern and welfare state. These principles give directives to the State to promote the welfare of people by providing basic facilities like shelter, food and clothing. The DPSPs embody the concept of a ‘welfare state’.

q:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Constitution of India defines its ‘basic structure’ in terms of federalism, secularism, fundamental rights and democracy.
2. The Constitution of India provides for ‘judicial review’ to safeguard the citizens’ liberties and to preserve the ideals on which the Constitution is based.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: There is no mention of the basic structure in the constitution of India, it is a judicial innovation. The Supreme Court of India in the famous Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala case (1973) ruled that the Parliament could amend any part of the Constitution but it should not alter or amend the basic structure or essential features of the Constitution. However, the Supreme Court did not define the term ‘basic structure’ and only listed a few principles — federalism, secularism, democracy — as being its part. The rule of law, Independence of the judiciary, doctrine of separation of powers, sovereign democratic republic, the parliamentary system of government, the principle of free and fair elections, welfare state, etc. are some of the components of the basic structure of the constitution. Article 13 (2) of the Constitution itself provides for the judicial review to safeguard the citizen’s liberties and rights. It prescribes that the Union or the States shall not make any law that takes away or abridges any of the fundamental rights and any law made in contravention of the aforementioned mandate.

q: One common agreement between Gandhism and Marxism is
opts: the final goal of a stateless society
expl: The final aim of both Gandhism and Marxism is the establishment of a stateless and classless society, but their means for achieving this aim of the stateless and classless society are different. Marxism favours violent means to achieve it, but Mahatma Gandhi wanted to achieve this end through non- violent means. Ways to achieve classless society: Marxism favours the overthrow of capitalism; social ownership of means of social production; compulsory labour while Ghandhism is in favour of the doctrine of trusteeship: moral persuasion of capitals to regard themselves as ‘trustee’ of public property; restore dignity of labour.

q: In the context of India, which one of the following is the characteristic appropriate for bureaucracy?
opts: An agency for widening the scope of parliamentary democracy.
An agency for strengthening the structure of federalism.
An agency for facilitating political stability and economic growth.
An agency or the implementation of public policy.
expl: Civil services or the bureaucracy constitutes the professionals who are permanent and salaried employees, and these employees are a part of the government’s executive organ. These bureaucrats are politically neutral and do their job to facilitate the effective working of different government departments and public policy implementations. However, these professionals work under the leadership of the ministers. Good policies and laws efficiently implemented by the civil servants can really serve their objectives.

q: The Preamble to the Constitution of India is
opts: a part of the Constitution but has no legal effect.
not a part of the Constitution and has no legal effect either.
a part of the Constitution and has the same legal effect as any other part.
a part of the Constitution but has no legal effect independently of other parts.
expl: We, The People of India, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a [Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic] and to secure to all its citizens: THE CONSTITUTION OF INDIA PREAMBLE JUSTICE, social, economic and political; of thought, expression, belief, LIBERTY faith and worship; of status and of opportunity EQUALITY and to promote among them all; assuring the dignity of FRATERNITY the individual and the [unity and integrity of the Nation]; IN OUR CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY this twenty-sixth day of November, 1949 do HEREBY ADOPT, ENACT AND GIVE TO OURSELVES THIS CONSTITUTION. 1. Subs, by the Constitution [Forty-second] Act, 1976, Sec.2, for ''Sovereign Democratic Republic'' (w.e.f.3.1.1977) 2. Subs, by the Constitution [Forty-second Amendment] Act, 1976, Sec.2, for ''Unity of the Nation'' (w.e.f.3.1.1977) Figure: Preamble of India The Preamble is the introductory part that contains the ideals, objectives and basic principles of the Constitution. The objectives under the Preamble are the key (directly and indirectly) to the evolution of the salient features of the Constitution. In Kesavananda Bharati Case (1973), the Supreme Court of India considered the Preamble as part of the Constitution. Again in 1995, in the Union Government vs LIC of India Case, the Supreme Court once again considered the Preamble an integral part of the constitution. One important point should be noted that the preamble (its objectives) is not directly enforceable in a court of law in India rather implemented through various acts and policies.

q: “Gold Tranche” (Reserve Tranche) refers to
opts: a loan system of the World Bank
one of the operations of a Central Bank
a credit system granted by WTO to its members
a credit system granted by IMF to its members
expl: The Gold Tranche or Reserve Tranche in the form of gold or foreign currency is the component of a member country’s quota with the International Monetary Fund (IMF). The member countries out of their quota get 25% in the form of foreign currency or gold as the reserve tranche or the gold tranche, and the remaining 75% in domestic currencies as the credit tranche. It is basically an emergency account that IMF members can access without agreeing to conditions or paying a service fee.

q:
With reference to the provisions contained in Part IV of the Constitution of India, which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. They shall be enforceable by courts.
2. They shall not be enforceable by any court.
3. The principles laid down in this part are to influence the making of laws by the State. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: The Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP) under Part IV (Articles 36–51) of the Constitution of India are non-justiciable or not enforceable by the courts for their violation while the Fundamental Rights under Part III (Articles 12-35) are justiciable or enforceable in the courts. The DPSP consists of all the ideals which the State should follow and keep in mind while formulating policies and enacting laws for the country. Article 37 under the DPSPs itself says that these principles are fundamental in the governance of the country, and the State should apply these principles in making laws. The DPSPs also help the courts in examining and determining the constitutional validity of a law.

q:
Consider the following statements:
1. According to the Constitution of India, a person who is eligible to vote can be made a minister in a State for six months even if he/she is not a member of the Legislature of that State.
2. According to the Representation of People Act, 1951, a person convicted of a criminal offence and sentenced to imprisonment for five years is permanently disqualified from contesting an election even after his release from prison.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: Article 164 of the Constitution allows a non-legislator to occupy a post in the council of ministers, including the office of the chief minister for six months, but within six months, he/she must become a member (either by election or by nomination) of either House of the state legislature, otherwise, he/ she ceases to be a minister. Moreover, to become a member of the state legislature and the legislative council one must be not less than 30 years and 25 years of age, respectively. Article 326 of the constitution provides that the elections to the House of the People and to the Legislative Assembly of every state shall be on the basis of adult suffrage (age not less than 18 years). Hence, statement 1 is not correct. According to the Section 8 (3) of the Representation of People Act, 1951, a person convicted of any offence and sentenced to imprisonment for not less than two years shall be disqualified for contesting an election (MLA or MP) from the date of such conviction and shall continue to be disqualified for a further period of six years since his release and not permanently.

q:
Consider the following statements:
1. The President of India can summon a session of the Parliament at such a place as he/she thinks fit.
2. The Constitution of India provides for three sessions of the Parliament in a year, but it is not mandatory to conduct all three sessions.
3. There is no minimum number of days that the Parliament is required to meet in a year.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: Article 85 (1) of the Constitution of India empowers the President to summon each House of Parliament to meet at such time and place as he thinks fit, there shall not be an interval more than six months between its last sitting in one session and the date appointed for its first sitting in the next session. It is a convention (not mentioned in the Constitution) that the Parliament meets for three sessions in a year. (a) The Budget Session (the beginning of the year) (b) A three-week Monsoon Session (from July to August) (c) The Winter Session (November- December) There is no minimum number of days that Parliament is required to meet in a year. However, several committees have recommended that Parliament should meet for at least 120 days in a year.

q:
Consider the following statements:
1. Aadhaar metadata cannot be stored for more than three months.
2. State cannot enter into any contract with private corporations for sharing Aadhaar data.
3. Aadhaar is mandatory for obtaining insurance products.
4. Aadhaar is mandatory for getting benefits funded out of the Consolidated Fund of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: Metadata or meta base is a set of data that describes and gives information about other data. As per the Regulation 27 (1) of the Authentication Regulations, the authentication records are not to be kept beyond a period of six months. According to a judgement of the Supreme Court of India in September 2018, the retaining of authentication data of citizens who have enrolled for Aadhaar beyond six months was “impermissible. The Supreme Court struck down the Aadhaar Regulation 26 (c) which allowed Unique Identification Authority of India (UIDAI) to store metadata for private firms, and the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) has directed insurance companies not to mandatorily ask for the Aadhaar details. The amendment made to Section 7 of the Aadhaar (Targeted Delivery of Financial and Other Subsidies, Benefits and Services) Act, 2016 has been upheld mandating Aadhar for beneficiaries for receipt of subsidy, benefit or service for which the expenditure is incurred from the Consolidated Fund of India.

q: Rajya Sabha has equal powers with Lok Sabha in
opts: the matter of creating new All India Services
expl: In the following matters, the powers and status of the Rajya Sabha are equal to that of the Lok Sabha: 1. Introduction and passage of Ordinary Bills. 2. Introduction and passage of Constitutional Amendment Bills. 3. Introduction and passage of Financial Bills involving expenditure from the Consolidated Fund of India. 4. Election and Impeachment of the President. 5. Election and removal of the Vice- President. 6. Making recommendation to the President for the removal of Chief Justice and judges of Supreme Court and high courts, Chief election commissioner and Comptroller and auditor general. 7. Approval of ordinances issued by the President. 8. Approval of proclamation of all three types of emergencies by the President.

q:
With reference to the funds under Members of Parliament Local Area Development Scheme (MPLADS), which of the following statements are correct?
1. MPLADS funds must be used to create durable assets like physical infrastructure for health, education, etc.
2. A specified portion of each MP’s fund must benefit SC/ST populations.
3. MPLADS funds are sanctioned on a yearly basis and the unused funds cannot be carried forward to the next year.
4. The district authority must inspect at least 10% of all works under implementation every year. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: Features of MPLAD (The Members of Parliament Local Area Development): MPLAD is a central sector scheme announced in December 1993. All works to meet locally felt infrastructure and development needs, with an emphasis on creation of durable assets in the constituency are permissible under MPLADS except those prohibited in Annexure-II of the guidelines of MPLAD. Development of Areas inhabited by Scheduled Caste and Scheduled Tribe: The Members of Parliament have to recommend every year at least 15% of the MPLADS entitlement for the year for areas inhabited by Scheduled Caste population and 7.5% for areas inhabited by Scheduled Tribe population. In case a State does not have STs inhabited areas, this amount may be utilized in SCs. MPLADS Funds are Non-lapsable: Funds left in the district can be carried forward for utilization in the subsequent years. Hence, statement 3 is not correct. Role of the District Authority: The District Authority would be responsible for overall coordination and supervision of the works under the scheme at the district level and inspect at least 10% of all works under implementation every year.

q: Which one of the following categories of Fundamental Rights incorporates protection against untouchability as a form of discrimination?
expl: There are six categories of Fundamental Rights under the Indian Constitution: 1. Right to Equality (Articles 14-18) 2. Right to Freedom (Articles 19-22) 3. Right against Exploitation (Articles 23-24) 4. Right to Freedom of Religion (Articles 25- 28) 5. Cultural and Educational Rights (Articles 29-30) 6. Right to Constitutional Remedies (Articles 32) Article 17, under the Rights to Equality (Articles 14-18), talks about the abolition of untouchability and prohibition of its practice. The enforcement of any disability arising out of untouchability shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law.

q: In India, separation of judiciary from the executive is enjoined by
opts: a Directive Principle of State Policy
expl: The Directive Principles of State Policy are enshrined in Part IV (Articles 36 to 51) of the Constitution. The separation of judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State is a directive under Article 50 of DPSPs. The separation of judiciary from the executive in pursuance of Article 50 is affected by the Criminal Procedure Code (1973).

q: Along with the Budget, the Finance Minister also places other documents before the Parliament which include ‘The Macro Economic Framework Statement’. The aforesaid document is presented because this is mandated by
opts: Article 112 and Article 110(1) of the Constitution of India
Article 113 of the Constitution of India
Provisions of the Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management Act, 2003
expl: Article 112 of the Indian Constitution defines the Union Budget of a year which is referred to as the Annual Financial Statement (AFS) which is an estimated receipts and expenditure of the Government in a financial year. Article 110 (1) under the constitution defines a ‘Money Bill’. Article 113 demands the inclusion of the estimates of expenditure (as Demands for Grants) from the Consolidated Fund of India in the Annual Financial Statement which is required to be voted by the Lok Sabha. This Act mandates the Central Government to lay before the Houses of Parliament, Macro- Economic Framework Statement, Medium Term Fiscal Policy Statement and Fiscal Policy Strategy Statement along with the Annual Financial Statement and Demands for Grants.

q: A constitutional government by definition is a
expl: A constitutional government is defined by the existence of a constitution accepted as the fundamental law of the polity that effectively controls the exercise of political power. The core component of the constitutional government is the existence of a “Rule-of Law” or a set of “basic laws” that binds both public office-holders and all members of a society (i.e. citizens) within a given territory. The core concept of the constitutionalism is the control of power by its distribution among the executive, legislative and judicial branches of government to balance the powers. The constitutional government operates within a set of legal and institutional constraints that both limits its power (limited government) and protects the individual liberty. A constitutional government can be a bi-party government, a monarchy and may not have the legislature.

q:
Other than the Fundamental Rights, which of the following parts of the Constitution of India reflect/reflects the principles and provisions of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948)?
1. Preamble
2. Directive Principles of State Policy
3. Fundamental Duties Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: The United Nations General Assembly (UNGA) in 1948 adopted and proclaimed the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR). The UDHR establishes the equality and dignity of every human being. It also stipulates that every government has a core duty to enable all people to enjoy all their inalienable rights and freedoms. The Universal Declaration of Human Rights: The preamble sets out the historical and social causes that led to the necessity of drafting the Declaration. Articles 1–2 Concepts of dignity, liberty and equality. Articles 3–5 The Right to life and the Prohibition of slavery and torture. Articles 6–11 legality of Human Rights with specific remedies. Articles 12–17 The Rights of the individual towards the community, including freedom of movement. Articles 18–21-sanctioned “constitutional liberties” . Articles 22–27 individual’s economic, social and cultural rights, including healthcare. Articles 28–30 general means of exercising these rights, the duty of the individual to society and the prohibition of the use of rights in contravention of the purposes of the United Nations Organization. We, The People of India, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a [Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic] and to secure to all its citizens: THE CONSTITUTION OF INDIA PREAMBLE JUSTICE, social, economic and political; of thought, expression, belief, LIBERTY faith and worship; of status and of opportunity EQUALITY and to promote among them all; assuring the dignity of FRATERNITY the individual and the [unity and integrity of the Nation]; IN OUR CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY this twenty-sixth day of November, 1949 do HEREBY ADOPT, ENACT AND GIVE TO OURSELVES THIS CONSTITUTION. 1. Subs, by the Constitution [Forty-second] Act, 1976, Sec.2, for ''Sovereign Democratic Republic'' (w.e.f.3.1.1977) 2. Subs, by the Constitution [Forty-second Amendment] Act, 1976, Sec.2, for ''Unity of the Nation'' (w.e.f.3.1.1977) Figure: Preamble of India Preamble: The objectives of Preamble such as Justice (social, economic and political), Equality and Liberty also reflect the principles of UDHR. Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP): Some of the DPSP that are in sync with human rights are as follows: Article 38: Promoting the welfare state Article 39: Minimizing inequalities Article 39A: Free legal aid Article 41: Supporting vulnerable sections of society like unemployed, sick, disabled and old age persons. Article 43: Securing living wage Fundamental duties (Article 51A): Article 51A (k) talks about providing opportunities for education to the child between the age of 6 and 14 years by the parents or guardian. This aspect somehow relates to ensuring the dignity of children through education.

q:
In India, Legal Services Authorities provide free legal services to which of the following type of citizens?
1. Person with an annual income of less than Rs. 1, 00, 000
2. Transgender with an annual income of less than Rs. 2, 00, 000
3. Member of Other Backward Classes (OBC) with an annual income of less than Rs. 3, 00, 000
4. All Senior Citizens Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: The Legal Services Authorities Act was enacted by the Parliament in 1987, which came into force on 9th November, 1995. The main objective of the Legal Services Authorities Act is to establish a nationwide uniform network for providing competent legal services to the weaker sections of the society on the basis of equal opportunity. The sections of the society enlisted under Section 12 of the Act are entitled for free legal services, they are: z z Women and children z z Members of SC/ST z z Industrial workmen z z Victims of disaster, violence, flood, drought, earthquake z z Disabled persons z z Persons in custody z z Victims of trafficking in human beings or beggar According to Section 12 (h) of the Act, those persons are also eligible for free legal services are: (a) whose annual income is less than ‘9000 or such other higher amount as may be prescribed by the State Government if the case is before a court other than the Supreme Court, and (b) whose income is less than 12000 or such other higher amount as may be prescribed by the Central Government, if the case is before the Supreme Court. Not all senior citizens are eligible for free legal aid, it is subject to the rules framed by the respective State Governments in this regard. It is not mentioned clearly whether a member of OBC with an annual income of less than 3,00,000 will avail free legal aid under the Act.

q:
Consider the following pairs: International Agreement/set-up Subject
1. Alma-Ata Declaration Healthcare of the people
2. Hague Convention Biological and chemical weapons
3. Talanoa Dialogue Global climate change
4. Under 2 Coalition Child rights Which of the pairs above is/are correctly matched?
expl: Alma-Ata Declaration: It was adopted at the International Conference on Primary Health Care (PHC) which was held in Almaty, Kazakhstan in 1978. It urged all the governments, health care workers and development workers to promote and protect the primary health of all the people. Hague Convention: There are a series of Hague Convention on different subjects such as Convention for the Protection of Cultural Property in the Event of Armed Conflict, Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction, etc. But, it is not related to biological and chemical weapons. Talanoa Dialogue: It was launched at the UN Climate Change Conference (COP 23) in Bonn (Germany) in 2017. The purpose of Talanoa is to share stories, build empathy and to make wise decisions for the collective good. Under2 Coalition: It is a Memorandum of Understanding by subnational governments to reduce their greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions towards net-zero by 2050. Currently, Telangana and Chhattisgarh are signatories to this pact from India.

q:
With reference to the history of India, consider the following pairs:
1. Aurang: In-charge of treasury of the State
2. Banian: Indian agent of the East India Company
3. Mirasidar: Designated revenue payer to the State Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
expl: Aurang: It is persian term for a warehouse where goods are collected before being sold. Bania: The word Bania (also Vania) is derived from the Sanskrit vanij, meaning ‘a merchant’. They acted as intermediaries for European merchants in Bengal during the British rule. Mirasdar: Mirasdar means hereditary landowner, co-proprietor. He was also a revenue payer to the state. In Southern India, the East India Company recognized mirasidars as the sole proprietors of land and dismissed tenants’ rights completely in the Ryotwari settlement system.

q:
With reference to the religious history of India, consider the following statements:
1. Sthaviravadins belong to Mahayana Buddhism.
2. Lokottaravadin sect was an offshoot of Mahasanghika sect of Buddhism.
3. The deification of Buddha by Mahasanghikas fostered Mahayana Buddhism.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: The Buddhist religion is majorly divided into Mahayana and Hinayana Sects. Hinayana is also called the Theravadins (Sanskrit: Sthaviras; Pali: Theras) and Theravada is also called Sthaviravada (Sect of the Elders). They don’t belong to Mahayana Buddhism. Mahasanghika (Great Congregation) was an early Buddhist school of thought (formed after the 2nd Buddhist Council in 383 BCE) separated itself from the Sthaviravada over differences in monastic practices (known as Vinaya). The Mahasanghikas’s views on the nature of the Buddha and the arhat (saint) acted as a precursor of the Mahayana form of Buddhism. The subdivisions of the Mahasanghikas over the next seven centuries included the Lokottaravadins, the Ekavyavaharikas and the Kaukkutikas.

q: Which of the following statements correctly explains the impact of the Industrial Revolution on India during the first half of the nineteenth century?
opts: Machines were introduced in the Indian textile industry in large numbers.
Railways lines were laid in many parts of the country.
Heavy duties were imposed on the imports of British manufactures.
expl: The Industrial Revolution brought severe consequences to the Indian society, and one of them was the decline and destruction of urban and rural handicraft industries. The discovery of steam power, inventions of the spinning mule and the power loom and its practice during the first half of the nineteenth century created a threat to the Indian textile industry. As a result, it greatly reduced the cost of British textile and made it internationally competitive. Heavy duties were imposed on the export of Indian textiles, Indian farmers were forced to produce cotton to support the British textile industry. Some positive effects of the Industrial Revolution: (a) The introduction of factories and assembly lines (b) Electricity development (c) Railroads (First train from Bombay to Thane in 1853) These developments took place mainly in the second half of the 19th century.

q:
Consider the following events in the history of India:
1. Rise of Pratiharas under King Bhoja
2. Establishment of Pallava power under Mahendravarman-I
3. Establishment of Chola power by Parantaka-I
4. Pala dynasty founded by Gopala What is the correct chronological order of the above events, starting from the earliest time?
expl: The Pallava dynasty existed from 275 CE to 897 CE, ruling a portion of southern India. Pallavas became a major power during the reign of Mahendravarman I (571–630 CE). The Pala dynasty reigned in Bihar and Bengal from the 8th to the 12th century. Its founder was Gopala (750-770 CE). Raja Mihir Bhoja (840–851 CE),] was the greatest of the Pratihara kings and battled against the Arab invaders. Under Bhoja (836- 885 CE) and his successor Mahendrapala I, the Pratihara Empire reached its peak of prosperity and power. The Chola Empire was founded by Vijayalaya. The reign of the Cholas began in the 9th century when they defeated the Pallavas to come into power. Parantaka I (reigned 907–953) laid the foundation of the kingdom and took the northern boundary up to Nellore (Andhra Pradesh). He defeated both the Pandyas and the Gangas.

q: Which of the following phrases defines the nature of the ‘Hundi’ generally referred to in the sources of the post-Harsha period?
opts: An advisory issued by the king to his subordinates
A diary to be maintained for daily accounts
An order from the feudal lord to his subordinates
expl: Hundis refer to financial instruments evolved on the Indian sub- continent used in trade and credit transactions. They were used: z z as remittance instruments (to transfer funds from one place to another), z z as credit instruments (to borrow money), z z for trade transactions (as bills of exchange) It facilitated easy transmission of money from one part of the country to another. By means of hundis, traders created credit which supplemented the money in circulation and financed commerce, particularly long- distance and international trade. The sarrafs (shroffs) who specialized in changing money also specialised in dealing with hundis.

q:
With reference to the book ‘Desher Katha’ written by Sakharam Ganesh Deuskar during the freedom struggle, consider the following statements:
1. It warned against the Colonial State’s hypnotic conquest of the mind.
2. It inspired the performance of swadeshi street plays and folk songs.
3. The use of ‘desh’ by Deuskar was in the specific context of the region of Bengal.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
expl: Sakharam Ganesh Deuskar, (1869-1912) was a close associate of Sri Aurobindo, wrote a book entitled Desher Katha describing in exhaustive detail the British commercial and industrial exploitation of India. He summarized the work of M. G. Ranade and Dadabhai Naoroji and warned against the Colonial rule as “hypnotic conquest of the mind”. This book captured the mind of young Bengal and assisted more than anything else in the preparation of the Swadeshi movement. Deuskar was the first to bring in the name of Swaraj and Sri Aurobindo was the first to endow it with its English equivalent, ‘Independence.’ It inspired the performance of swadeshi street plays and folk songs.

q:
The Gandhi-Irwin Pact included which of the following?
1. Invitation to Congress to participate in the Round Table Conference.
2. Withdrawal of Ordinances promulgated in connection with the Civil Disobedience Movement.
3. Acceptance of Gandhiji’s suggestion for enquiry into police excesses.
4. Release of only those prisoners who were not charged with violence. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: The Delhi Pact, also known as the Gandhi-Irwin Pact of 5 March 1931. Lord Irwin, the Viceroy and Governor-General of India (1926-1931), on behalf of the British government, agreed with Mahatma Gandhi on the following: z z immediate release of all political prisoners not convicted of violence; z z remission of all fines not yet collected; z z return of all lands not yet sold to third parties; z z lenient treatment to those government servants who had resigned; z z right to make salt in coastal villages for personal consumption (not for sale); z z right to peaceful and non-aggressive picketing; and z z withdrawal of emergency ordinances. The Viceroy turned down two of Gandhiji’s demands, one on the public inquiry into police excesses and the second on the commutation of death sentence of Bhagat Singh and his comrades to life sentence. Gandhiji on behalf of the Congress agreed to suspend the civil disobedience movement and to participate in the next Round Table Conference.

q: The Vital-Vidhvansak, the first monthly journal to have the untouchable people as its target audience was published by
expl: Gopal Baba Walangkar (1840- 1900) was born into a family of the untouchable Mahar caste in the Raigad district of Maharashtra. He was also known as Gopal Krishna. He developed a racial theory to explain the oppression. He in 1888 published the first journal titled Vital-Vidhvansak (Destroyer of Brahmanical or Ceremonial Pollution) targeted at the untouchable people. He was the first to fight for the rights of the Mahars in Maharashtra.

q: With reference to the history of India, the terms ‘kulyavapa’ and ‘dronavapa’ denote
expl: In the Gupta era, the terms Kulyavapa, Dronavapa and Adhavapa were related to land measurements mainly mentioned in the copper plate inscriptions that were found in Bengal. Texts and inscriptions during the Gupta period mention various land measure terms: z z The angula (probably ¾ inch) was the smallest measure. z z The hasta (cubit) was the standardized distance between the tip of the elbow and the middle finger (18 inches). z z Larger units of measure included the dhanu/danda and nala. These words indicate the area of land that was required to sow seed grains of the weight respectively of one kulya, drona and adhaka. The element “vapa” means the act of the act of sowing (throwing or scattering).

q: Who among the following rulers advised his subjects through this inscription? “Whosoever praises his religious sect or blames other sects out of excessive devotion to his own sect, with the view of glorifying his own sect, he rather injures his own sect very severely.”
expl: Ashoka condemns the practice of elevating one’s own religion at the expense of someone else’s in his Edict XII (or 12th Rock Edict). The 12th Rock Edict read: Whoever praises his own religion, due to excessive devotion and condemns others with the thought ‘Let me glorify my own religion’, only harms his own religion. One should listen to and respect the doctrines professed by others.

q:
What are the advantages of fertigation in agriculture?
1. Controlling the alkalinity of irrigation water is possible.
2. Efficient application of Rock Phosphate and all other phosphatic fertilizers is possible.
3. Increased availability of nutrients to plants is possible.
4. Reduction in the leaching of chemical nutrients is possible. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: The practice of supplying crops in the field with fertilizers via irrigation is called fertigation. It is a modern agro-technique in which the efficiency of the fertilizer is increased and minimizing the amount of application that facilitates higher yield and reduced environmental pollution. The benefits of fertigation methods include: Increased nutrients absorption by plants. Accurate placement of nutrient, where the water goes the nutrient goes as well. Ability to ‘microdose’, feeding. Reduction of fertilizer, chemicals and water needed that leads to reduced leaching of chemicals. Reduced water consumption due to the plant’s increased root mass’s ability to trap and hold water. Application of nutrients can be controlled at the precise time and rate necessary. Through fertigation, pH of the irrigation water can be managed. Disadvantages of fertigation z z Uneven nutrient distribution occurs when the irrigation system is faulty. z z Chemical reactions of fertilizer with calcium and magnesium, bicarbonates in water, which can lead to chemical clogging. z z Suitable for readily soluble or liquid fertilizers. Phosphatic fertilizer and some micronutrients may precipitate in micro- irrigation system. z z Corrosion resistant fertigation equipments are needed. z z Potential chemical backflow into the water supply source.

q:
Consider the following minerals:
1. Bentonite
2. Chromite
3. Kyanite
4. Sillimanite In India, which of the above is/are officially designated as major minerals?
expl: According to the National Account Statistics (2007), minerals are broadly classified into major and minor minerals. Major Minerals include the following: Fuel Minerals: Coal, Lignite, Natural Gas, Petroleum (Crude). Metallic Minerals: Bauxite, Chromite, Copper Ore, Gold, Iron Ore, Lead (Concentrates), Zinc (Concentrates), Manganese Ore, Silver, Tin (Concentrates), Tungsten (Concentrates). Non-Metallic Minerals: Agate andalusite, Apatite, Asbestos, Ball Clay, Barytes, Calcite, Chalk, Clay, Corundum, Calcarious sand and Diamond, etc. Minor Minerals include Bentonite, Boulder, Brick Earth, Building Stones, Chalcedony or Corundum, Fuller’s Earth, Gravel and Lime Stone, etc. Chromite, Kyanite and Sillimanite are major minerals, whereas Bentonite is a minor mineral.

q:
With reference to Ocean Mean Temperature (OMT), which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. OMT is measured up to a depth of 26ºC isotherm which is 129 meters in the south-western Indian Ocean during January-March.
2. OMT collected during January-March can be used in assessing whether the amount of rainfall in monsoon will be less or more than a certain long-term mean. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: The Sea Surface Temperature (SST) was used for predicting the total amount of rainfall that India receives during the monsoon season. The Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology (IITM), Pune scientists have found that OMT (Ocean Mean Temperature) is a better alternative to predict the Indian summer monsoon than the Sea Surface Temperature. The SST is restricted to a few millimetres of the top ocean layer while the OMT is measured up to a depth of 26°C isotherm, is more stable and consistent. The 26°C isotherm is seen at depths varying from 50-100 metres while the mean 26°C isotherm depth during January-March in the Southwestern Indian Ocean is 59 metres. The OMT collected during January-March can be used for whether the amount of monsoon rainfall will be more or less than the long-term mean.

q:
With reference to chemical fertilizers in India, consider the following statements:
1. At present, the retail price of chemical fertilizers is market-driven and not administered by the Government.
2. Ammonia, which is an input of urea, is produced from natural gas.
3. Sulphur, which is a raw material for phosphoric acid fertilizer, is a by-product of oil refineries.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: The Government of India subsidizes fertilizers to ensure that fertilizers are easily available to farmers for the country to remain self-sufficient in agriculture production. The government also controls the price of fertilizer and the amount of production. Ammonia (NH3) has been synthesized from natural gas. In this process, natural gas molecules are reduced to carbon and hydrogen. The hydrogen is then purified and reacted with nitrogen to produce ammonia. This synthetic ammonia is used as fertilizer, either directly as ammonia or indirectly after synthesis as urea, ammonium nitrate and monoammonium or diammonium phosphates. Sulfur is a major by-product of oil refining and gas processing. Sulfuric acid is used in the production of both Monoammonium Phosphate (MAP) and Diammonium Phosphate (DAP).

q:
With reference to India’s Desert National Park, which of the following statements are correct?
1. It is spread over two districts.
2. There is no human habitation inside the Park.
3. It is one of the natural habitats of the Great Indian Bustard. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: Kishwar Desert N.P. Sarlska Ranthambore Rann of Kutch Pench Valavadar Gir Vansda Ikshawaka Molen Dandeli Nagarhole Guindy Hemis High Altitude Great Himalyas Valley of Flowers Corbett Dudhwa Shivpuri Valavadar Sanjay Gandhi Silent Valley Indira Gandhi N.P. Dandipur Kankar Valley Indravati Nagzari Bandhavgarh Kanchanjunga Nau Daha Sirohi Keibul Lamjao Kanha Kili Karisanga REFERENCES INDIA– Political 1: 43.16 million 0 km100 400 km International Boundary State Boundary Country Capital State Headquarter Jammu and Kashmir Island Islands I. Is. J & K Scale This map is not to scale and is for reference only. Figure: National parks of India The Thar Desert or the Desert National Park (DNP) is a unique and only habitat of its type in the Indian subcontinent. z z It is bordered by the Aravalli hills in the east, fertile plains of the Indus in the west, the Great Rann of Kutch in the south and the riparian sub Himalayan plains in the north. z z It covers an area of 3162 km² of which 1900 km² is in Jaisalmer district and remaining 1262 km² is in Barmer district of Rajasthan State. z z DNP is a home to Great Indian Bustard (locally called Godawan) along with more than 100 species of birds. z z The Thar desert is the most thickly populated desert in the world with an average density of 83 persons/km² (compared to 7 km² of other deserts), but the human population within the DNP is low.

q: Siachen Glacier is situated to the
expl: The Siachen Glacier is located in the Eastern Karakoram range in the Himalayas. It is located just northeast of Point NJ9842 where the Line of Control between India and Pakistan ends. It is the largest glacier outside the polar and subpolar regions. It lies to the west of Aksai Chin, north of the Nubra valley and almost east of Gilgit.

q:
With reference to the history of India, consider the following pairs: Famous Place Present State
1. Bhilsa Madhya Pradesh
2. Dwarasamudra Maharashtra
3. Girinagar Gujarat
4. Sthanesvara Uttar Pradesh Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
expl: Vidisha is a city in the state of Madhya Pradesh located around 60 km northeast of the state capital, Bhopal. Its former names are Bhelsa and Besnagar in ancient times. It is an archaeologically important site. Halebidu, previously known as Dorasamudra or Dwarasamudra, the ancient Hoysala capital, houses the ornate Hoysaleswara and Kedareswara temples and is one of the well- visited tourist attractions of Karnataka state. Girnar, also known as Girinagar (city-on- the-hill) or Revatak Parvata, is a group of mountains in the Junagadh District of Gujarat. It is a sacred place for Jains because here Lord Niminatha went to attain salvation. Thanesar or Sthanesvara is located adjacent to Kurukshetra city in Haryana.

q:
Consider the following statements:
1. 36% of India’s districts are classified as ‘overexploited’ or ‘critical’ by the Central Ground Water Authority (CGWA).
2. CGWA was formed under the Environment (Protection) Act.
3. India has the largest area under groundwater irrigation in the world.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: Areas across the country based on groundwater levels are split into three categories: (a) Over-exploited (extraction is more than 100%) (b) Critical (extraction is 90-100%) (c) Semi-critical (extraction rate is 70%-90%) As per the report ‘National Compilation on Dynamic Groundwater Resources of India, 2017’ of CGWA, 17% of the units have been categorized as ‘Over-Exploited’, 5% are ‘Critical’, and 14% are semi-critical. The Central Ground Water Authority (CGWA) was constituted under Section 3 (3) of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. It was established to regulate and control the development and management of ground water resources. As per the report of Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) of UN, the countries with the largest area under groundwater irrigation are India (39 million ha), China (19 million ha) and the USA (17 million ha).

q:
Consider the following statements:
1. Jet streams occur in the Northern Hemisphere only.
2. Only some cyclones develop an eye.
3. The temperature inside the eye of a cyclone is nearly 10°C lesser than that of the surroundings.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: Polar Cell Polar Jet Northern Ferrel Cell Subtropical Jet Tropical Easterly Jet Northern Hadley Cell Southern Hadley Cell Southern Ferrel Cell Polar Jet Polar Cell Subtropical Jet Low SW Monsoon Somali Jet SE Trades Mascerene High Westerlies L Figure: Major jet streams Jet Stream is a geostrophic wind blowing horizontally generally from west to east at an altitude of 20,000-50,000 feet through the upper layers of the troposphere. Jet Streams develop where air masses of different temperatures meet; it means the greater the difference in temperature, the faster the wind velocity inside the jet stream. It extend from 20° latitude to the poles in both hemispheres. Cyclones are of two types, tropical cyclone and temperate cyclone. The centre of a tropical cyclone is known as the ‘eye’. The wind remains calm at the centre with no rainfall. However, temperate cyclone does not have eye. The warmest temperatures are found in the eye itself, not in the eyewall clouds where the latent heat occurs.

q: Among the following Tiger Reserves, which one has the largest area under “Critical Tiger Habitat”?
expl: The Wild Life Protection Act, 1972 provides the identification of the Critical Tiger Habitats (CTH) on the basis of scientific evidence. It states that “such areas are required to be kept as inviolative for the purpose of tiger conservation, without affecting the rights of the Scheduled Tribes or such other forest dwellers”. These habitats are notified by the state government in consultation with the expert committee constituted for the purpose. Area of the core/critical tiger habitat: Corbett (Uttarakhand): 821. 99 sq. kms Ranthambore (Rajasthan): 1113. 36 sq. kms Sundarbans (West Bengal): 1699. 62 sq. kms Nagarjunsagar Srisailam (part of Andhra Pradesh): 2595. 72 sq. kms

q: If a particular plant species is placed under Schedule VI of The Wildlife Protection Act, 1972, what is the implication?
opts: A licence is required to cultivate that plant.
Such a plant cannot be cultivated under any circumstances.
It is a Genetically Modified crop plant.
Such a plant is invasive and harmful to the ecosystem.
expl: The Wild Life Protection Act, 1972 is enacted for protection of plants wild animal species and birds, wild animal species and birds. It has six schedules which give varying degrees of protection. Schedule I and part II of Schedule II provide absolute protection and highest penalties for offences. Species listed in Schedule III and Schedule IV are also protected, but the penalties are much lower. Schedule V includes the animals which may be hunted. The Schedule VI contains specified endemic plants which are prohibited from cultivation and planting. In Schedule VI, following plants are included: z z Beddomes’ cycad (Cycas beddomei), z z Blue Vanda (Vanda soerulec), z z Kuth (Saussurea lappa), z z Ladies slipper orchids (Paphiopedilum spp.), z z Pitcher plant (Nepenthes khasiana), z z Red Vanda (Rananthera imschootiana) However, the further also states that cultivation of specified plants without licence is prohibited.

q: With reference to the period of Gupta dynasty in ancient India, the towns Ghantasala, Kadura and Chaul were known as
opts: places of exquisite stone art and architecture
expl: During the ancient period, India had trade and cultural contacts with Egypt, Rome, Greeks, Arabs, China and almost all Southeast Asian countries, and the trade was carried out through ports. The ports on the west coast were Barygaya, Suppara, Calliena, Semylla, Mandagore, Palaepatme, Malizigara, Aurranobbas, Naura, Tyndis, Muziris and Nelcynda. The ports on the east coast were Tamralipti, Charitrapur, Paluru, Dantapur, Kalingapatnam, Pithunda, Sopatma, Ghantasala, Kadura, Poduca, Puhar, Korkai and Camara. The trade with the Roman Empire was done through the ports of Kalyuan, Chaul, Broach and Cambay in the western India. Chaul was one of the famous ports of the Gupta dynasty. Kadura and Ghantasala were the ports of the Andhra region while Kaveripattanam (Puhar) and Tondail were the important ports of the Pandya region.

q:
What is/are the advantage/advantages of zero tillage in agriculture?
1. Sowing of wheat without burning the residue of previous crop.
2. Without the need for nursery of rice saplings, direct planting of paddy seeds in the wet soil is possible.
3. Carbon sequestration in the soil is possible. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: Zero Tillage is the process of agriculture that promotes conservation agriculture. It is also called no-till farming. It is a cultivation technique in which the soil is disturbed only along the slit or in the hole into which the seeds are planted, the reserved detritus from previous crops covers and protects the seedbed. The direct seeding of wheat into unploughed soil and with rice residues left behind has proved very beneficial. Direct Seeded Rice (DSR) is a viable option to reduce the unproductive water flows. It refers to the process of establishing a rice crop from seeds sown in the field rather than by transplanting seedlings from the nursery. No tilled soils are cooler than others because a surface layer of plant residues is present and helps in the sequestration of carbon in the soil enhancing its quality, reducing the threat of global warming.

q:
According to India’s National Policy on Biofuels, which of the following can be used as raw materials for the production of biofuels?
1. Cassava
2. Damaged wheat grains
3. Groundnut seeds
4. Horse gram
5. Rotten potatoes
6. Sugar beet Select the correct answer using the code given below:
expl: The National Policy on Biofuels, 2018, allows the production of ethanol from damaged food grains which are unfit for human consumption like wheat, broken rice, etc. The approval of the National Biofuel Coordination Committee is necessary in making of ethanol from food grains. The Policy allows use of sugarcane juice, sugar containing materials like sugar beet, sweet sorghum, starch containing materials like corn, cassava, damaged food grains like wheat, broken rice, rotten potatoes, unfit for human consumption hence they are used for ethanol production.

q: Which of the following statements best describes the term ‘Social Cost of Carbon’? It is a measure, in monetary value, of the
opts: long-term damage done by a tonne of CO2 emissions in a given year.
requirement of fossil fuels for a country to provide goods and services to its citizens, based on the burning of those fuels.
efforts put in by a climate refugee to adapt to live in a new place.
contribution of an individual person to the carbon footprint on the planet Earth.
expl: The Social Cost of Carbon (SCC) is an estimate of the economic damages in dollars that would result from emitting an additional ton of greenhouse gases into the atmosphere. It puts the effects of climate change into economic terms. It will help the policymakers to understand the economic impacts of decisions that would increase or decrease emissions. India’s country-level SCC emission was estimated to be one of the highest at $86 per tonne of CO2, followed by the US ($48) and Saudi Arabia ($47).

q:
With reference to pulse production in India, consider the following statements:
1. Black gram can be cultivated as both kharif and rabi crop.
2. Green-gram alone accounts for nearly half of pulse production.
3. In the last three decades, while the production of kharif pulses has increased, the production of rabi pulses has decreased.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: In India, the important pulse crops grown in winter (rabi) are chickpea, lentil, lathyrus, field pea and kidney bean while green gram, black gram and cowpea are grown in both spring and rainy season. Black gram is a warm weather crop. It is grown up in areas receiving an annual rainfall ranging from 600 to 1000 mm. It can be grown in all the seasons, the majority of black gram cultivation falls in either rabi or late rabi seasons particularly in peninsular India. According to the Directorate of Economics and Statistics (DES), the share of pulse production in 2018-19 was Tur (15. 34%), Gram (43. 29%), Moong (green gram,10. 04%), Urad (black gram, 13. 93%), Lentil (6. 67%) and Other Pulses (10%). In the last three decades, the production of kharif pulses and rabi pulses have increased.

q: “The crop is subtropical in nature. A hard frost is injurious to it. It requires at least 210 frost-free days and 50 to 100 centimeters of rainfall for its growth. A light well-drained soil capable of retaining moisture is ideally suited for the cultivation of the crop.” Which one of the following is that crop?
expl: Cotton: Temperature: Between 21-30°C, 210 frost free days. Rainfall: Around 50-100 cm. Soil Type: Well-drained black cotton soil of Deccan Plateau. Jute: Temperature: Between 15-34°C Rainfall: Around 100-250 cm. Soil Type: In all kinds of soils from clay to sandy loam, but loamy alluvial is the best one. Sugarcane: Temperature: Between 28-32°C Rainfall: Around 75-120 cm. Soil Type: It can grow in black cotton soils, loams, brown or reddish loams, clayey loams and even laterites. Tea: Temperature: Between 20-30°C. Rainfall: Around 150-300 cm. Soil Type: Humus and organic matter rich, deep and fertile well-drained soil.

q:
With reference to solar water pumps, consider the following statements:
1. Solar power can be used for running surface pumps and not for submersible pumps.
2. Solar power can be used for running centrifugal pumps and not the ones with piston.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: The main components in a solar pumping system include a photovoltaic (PV) array, an electric motor and a pump. There are several different types of solar- powered pumps, but primarily four types of solar water pumps are there - submersible pumps, surface pumps, direct current (DC) pumps and alternate current (AC) pumps. Both centrifugal as well as piston pumps can run on solar power.

q:
With reference to the current trends in the cultivation of sugarcane in India, consider the following statements:
1. A substantial saving in seed material is made when ‘bud chip settlings’ are raised in a nurse and transplanted in the main field.
2. When direct planting of setts is done, the germination percentage is better with single-budded setts as compared to setts with many buds.
3. If bad weather conditions prevail when setts are directly planted, single-budded setts have better survival as compared to large setts.
4. Sugarcane can be cultivated using settlings prepared from tissue culture.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
expl: Tissue culture technology: In this technique, fragments of plants are cultured and grown in a laboratory. It provides a new way to rapidly produce and supply disease-free seed cane of existing commercial varieties. It uses meristem to clone the mother plant and preserves the genetic identity. The tissue culture technique is turning out to be uneconomical. Bud chip technology: It is an effective alternative of tissue culture and reduces the mass and enables quick multiplication of seeds. This method is more economical and convenient, and the returns are relatively better, with substantial savings on the seed material used for planting. The researchers have found that the setts having two buds are giving germination about 65 to 70% with better yield. Large setts have better survival under bad weather, but single-budded setts also give 70% germination if protected with chemical treatment.

q:
In the context of India, which of the following is/are considered to be practice(s) of eco-friendly agriculture?
1. Crop diversification
2. Legume intensification
3. Tensiometer use
